request.question: Who wrote the debut song on the debut album of the artist who also released the album "I'm Still Here"?
request.answer: Billy Henderson and Steven Dale Jones

title: Neon Hitch discography
The Love Song (Jeff Bates song)
Crazy Over You
A Little More Country Than That
Mindy McCready
You Won't Ever Be Lonely (song)
Ten Thousand Angels (song)
Drunker Than Me
A Better Man
What I Really Meant to Say
sentences: The discography of British singer and songwriter Neon Hitch.
Neon's debut digital single "Get Over U" was released in February 2011.
Hitch then released her single "Bad Dog", which was intended to be the lead single from her debut album.
Later in 2011, she was featured on Gym Class Heroes' song "Ass Back Home".
In 2012, Neon Hitch released "Fuck U Betta" and Gold" featuring Tyga, her official first and second singles respectively.
Both songs peaked at number one on the "Billboard" Dance/Club Play chart.
Neon then debuted an EP entitled "Happy Neon" in January 2013, which was released online for free.
In October 2013, Neon announced that her debut album "Beg, Borrow & Steal" had been scrapped and she would release a new album that had more of her soul in it.
In January 2014, Neon released the mixtape "301 to Paradise" for free.

In May 2014, it was announced that Hitch had parted ways with her label Warner Bros. and was gearing up to releasing her new debut album "Eleutheromaniac"; she also released "Happy Neon" and "301 to Paradise" to digital retailers independently in the same month.
She premiered the lead single of "Eleutheromaniac", "Yard Sale", in August 2014.
In January 2015, Neon released "Sparks" as the first single from the album.
In March 2015, Hitch released the EP "24:00" for free.
In 2016, it was announced that Hitch had changed the name of her debut album to "Anarchy", which was released on July 22, 2016.
The lead single from the album, "Please", was released on July 8, 2016.
The album did not contain any of the singles intended to be on "Eluetheromaniac", but included a promomotional single she released in 2015, "Freedom".
"The Love Song" is the title of a debut song co-written and recorded by American country music Jeff Bates.
It was released in December 2002 as the lead-off single from his debut album "Rainbow Man", released on RCA Nashville.
The song was also Bates' highest-charting single on the Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart, reaching a peak of #8.
Bates wrote this song with Kenny Beard and Casey Beathard.
"Crazy Over You" is a debut song recorded by American country music duo Foster & Lloyd, who also wrote the song.
It was released in May 1987 as the first single from their self-titled debut album.
It was their most successful single, peaking at #4 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart in 1987.
The song was simultaneously recorded and released by Ricky Van Shelton on his debut album, "Wild-Eyed Dream".
"A Little More Country Than That" is a debut song written by Joey + Rory's Rory Lee Feek, Wynn Varble and Don Poythress, and recorded by American country music artist Easton Corbin.
It was released in August 2009 as the first single from his self-titled debut album.
In April 2010, the song reached Number One on the country music charts, making Corbin the first male solo artist in seven years to send a debut single to Number One.
It has been certified Gold by the RIAA.
Malinda Gayle "Mindy" McCready (November 30, 1975 – February 17, 2013) was an American country music singer.
Active from 1995 until her death in 2013, she recorded a total of five studio albums.
Her debut album, 1996's "Ten Thousand Angels", was released on BNA Records and was certified 2× Platinum by the RIAA, while 1997's "If I Don't Stay the Night" was certified Gold.
1999's "I'm Not So Tough", her final album for BNA, was less successful, and she left the label.
A self-titled fourth album followed in 2002 on Capitol Records.
McCready's fifth and final studio album, "I'm Still Here", was released in March 2010 on Iconic Records.
"You Won't Ever Be Lonely" is a debut song co-written and recorded by American country music singer Andy Griggs.
It was released in November 1998 as his debut single, and was served as the lead-off single and title track from his debut album "You Won't Ever Be Lonely".
It peaked at number 2 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks charts.
Griggs wrote the song with Brett Jones.
"Ten Thousand Angels" is a debut song written by Billy Henderson and Steven Dale Jones, and recorded by American country music artist Mindy McCready.
It was released on April 1, 1996 as the first single and title track from the album "Ten Thousand Angels".
The song reached #6 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart.
"Drunker Than Me" is the title of a debut song co-written and recorded by American country music artist Trent Tomlinson.
It was released in October 2005 as the first single from his debut album "Country Is My Rock".
Tomlinson wrote the song with Ashe Underwood.
"A Better Man" is title of a debut song co-written and performed by American country music artist Clint Black.
It was released in February 1989 as his debut single, and was served as the first single from his debut album, "Killin' Time".
It was written by Black and Hayden Nicholas.

When "A Better Man" went to No. 1 on the "Billboard magazine" Hot Country Singles chart on June 10, Black was the first artist since Freddy Fender to ascend to the top of the country chart with his first charted single.
In addition, "A Better Man" was the No. 1 song of 1989 on the Hot Country Singles chart.
The success of "A Better Man," along with the follow-up "Killin' Time," were instrumental in Black winning the Country Music Association's Horizon Award in 1989.
"What I Really Meant to Say" is a debut song written by Cyndi Thomson, Tommy Lee James and Chris Waters.
It was released in March 2001 as Thomson's debut single, as well as the lead-off single from her debut album "My World".
The song reached the top of the Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart, giving Thomson her only Number One single on that chart.
It also made her the third country music artist that year to reach Number One with a debut single.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.question: Who wrote the debut song on the debut album of the artist who also released the album "I'm Still Here"?
response.gold_answer: Billy Henderson and Steven Dale Jones

response.hop1_query: Who wrote the debut song on the debut album of the artist who also released the album "I'm Still Here"?
hop1_docs: Cold (But I'm Still Here) | "Cold (But I'm Still Here)" is the first single off Evans Blue's debut album, "The Melody and the Energetic Nature of Volume".  The song was released December 13, 2005, two months prior to the album release, and garnered frequent radio play in anticipation of the upcoming album.  Evans Blue's official MySpace explains the meaning of this song as well as others by the group.  A music video was also produced for "Cold (But I'm Still Here)."  The song currently has over three million plays on MySpace.

Kick Back Relax | "Kick Back Relax" is a pop song recorded by Swedish singer and Idol winner Agnes taken from her second album "Stronger".  The track was written by Jörgen Elofsson who also wrote her debut single "Right Here Right Now".  It was released as the album's first single in September 2006.

Crazy Over You | "Crazy Over You" is a debut song recorded by American country music duo Foster & Lloyd, who also wrote the song.  It was released in May 1987 as the first single from their self-titled debut album.  It was their most successful single, peaking at #4 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart in 1987.  The song was simultaneously recorded and released by Ricky Van Shelton on his debut album, "Wild-Eyed Dream".

Outta Here (song) | "Outta Here" is the title song of the debut studio album by Dutch R&B singer-songwriter Esmée Denters.  It was released as the debut single both from the artist and the album in the Netherlands on 14 April 2009, 27 April in New Zealand followed by 16 August in the UK.

Gabriela (singer) | Her self-titled debut album was released in 2001; it was produced by Vehnee Saturno, who also wrote eight of the album's ten songs.  Another of the songs was a rendition of Bryan Adams's "Please Forgive Me".  Her second album, "In the Interim", was released in 2004.

Anywhere but Here (song) | "Anywhere but Here" is the title of the fourth single released by American country music artist Sammy Kershaw.  It was co-written by Bob DiPiero and John Scott Sherrill with Buddy Cannon, who also produced it.  It was released in September 1992 as the final single from his debut album, "Don't Go Near the Water".  It peaked at #10 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart and #17 on the Canadian "RPM" country singles chart.

Dollhouse (Melanie Martinez song) | "Dollhouse" is the debut single by American recording artist Melanie Martinez.  The song was featured on Martinez's debut EP "Dollhouse" (2014), where it was released as the EP's lead single on February 9, 2014.  "Dollhouse" was later featured on Martinez's debut studio album, "Cry Baby" (2015).  Lyrically, "Dollhouse" is about a dysfunctional family, who, according to Martinez, is "hiding being a perfect plastic facade".  Martinez has also stated that "Dollhouse" is a metaphor for how people view celebrities and their seemingly perfect public lives.  Dollhouse is also the second song of Cry Baby, and this song is also the first official song Melanie Martinez wrote.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: debut singles/albums and songwriting credits for named artists in the passages.

Facts supported verbatim by the passages:
- Evans Blue — "'Cold (But I'm Still Here)' is the first single off Evans Blue's debut album, 'The Melody and the Energetic Nature of Volume'"; "The song was released December 13, 2005."
- Melanie Martinez — "'Dollhouse' is the debut single by American recording artist Melanie Martinez"; "'Dollhouse' ... is the first official song Melanie Martinez wrote"; featured on debut studio album "'Cry Baby' (2015)" and EP "'Dollhouse' (2014)"; lead single released "February 9, 2014."
- Foster & Lloyd — "'Crazy Over You' is a debut song recorded by ... Foster & Lloyd, who also wrote the song"; released "May 1987" as first single from their self-titled debut album.
- Esmée Denters — "'Outta Here' is the title song of the debut studio album by Dutch R&B singer-songwriter Esmée Denters"; released "14 April 2009."
- Agnes — "'Kick Back Relax' ... was written by Jörgen Elofsson who also wrote her debut single 'Right Here Right Now'"; from second album "'Stronger'."
- Gabriela — "Her self-titled debut album was released in 2001; it was produced by Vehnee Saturno, who also wrote eight of the album's ten songs."
- Sammy Kershaw — "'Anywhere but Here' ... released in September 1992 as the final single from his debut album, 'Don't Go Near the Water'"; "co-written by Bob DiPiero and John Scott Sherrill with Buddy Cannon."

Ambiguities / missing information blocking the question:
- No passage states an album titled exactly "I'm Still Here" — MISSING: the artist (bridge entity) who released an album titled "I'm Still Here".
- Passage [1] contains "'Cold (But I'm Still Here)'" (Evans Blue) — this is a similarly worded song title but not an album titled "I'm Still Here" (potential false lead).

Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
1) Search/fetch the Wikipedia disambiguation or article for "I'm Still Here (album)" (query: "I'm Still Here (album) - Wikipedia"); inspect disambiguation candidates.
2) For the identified artist page (once found), fetch "Artist Name - Wikipedia" and inspect lede/infobox and the album page "I'm Still Here (Artist Name album)" (sections: Track listing, Singles, Personnel/Credits).
3) If no album page, search for "I'm Still Here (album) 'album' site:wikipedia.org" and check pages/sections for songwriting or single credits (look for "Singles", "Track listing", "Songwriting", "Credits").
4) If multiple artists share the album title, fetch each candidate artist's debut album page and check their debut album's lead single and songwriting credit (sections: "Singles", "Track listing", "Personnel").
hop2_docs: I'm Still Here (album) | I'm Still Here is the fifth and final studio album by American country music artist Mindy McCready.  It was released on March 23, 2010, via Linus Entertainment.  The album includes the single "I'm Still Here," as well as re-recordings of her 1996 singles "Guys Do It All the Time" and "Ten Thousand Angels."  Mindy performed on "Fox & Friends" to promote the album in April 2010.  This is McCready's final release before her death in 2013.

You're Still Here | "You're Still Here" is a song recorded by American country music artist Faith Hill.  It was released in June 2003 as the fourth single from her fifth album "Cry" (2002).  The song reached #28 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart.  The song was written by Matraca Berg and Aimee Mayo.
I'm Still Here... Damn It! | I'm Still Here... Damn It is an album released by singer/comedian/actress Sandra Bernhard.  The album is a live recording of her one-woman show of the same title;

Cold (But I'm Still Here) | "Cold (But I'm Still Here)" is the first single off Evans Blue's debut album, "The Melody and the Energetic Nature of Volume".  The song was released December 13, 2005, two months prior to the album release, and garnered frequent radio play in anticipation of the upcoming album.  Evans Blue's official MySpace explains the meaning of this song as well as others by the group.  A music video was also produced for "Cold (But I'm Still Here)."  The song currently has over three million plays on MySpace.

Still Here (John Mark Nelson album) | Still Here is the first full-length album released by John Mark Nelson on 29 December 2011.  The songs for this album were crafted when Nelson was 17 years of age.  The album was featured on the local radio stations of Minnesota.  After this album, Nelson decided to stop his music career for a short time.
I'm Still Here (Mandisa song) | "I'm Still Here" is the second single, released in 2017, from Mandisa's fifth studio album, "Out Of The Dark".

Heart Beats (Dami Im album) | Heart Beats is the second studio album by Australian recording artist Dami Im, released on 14 October 2014 by Sony Music Australia.  While Im's previous self-titled debut album consisted of cover songs she performed on "The X Factor Australia", "Heart Beats" features a mixture of original ballads and up-tempo pop songs.  Im worked on the album with many songwriters and producers, including Robby De Sa, Jon Hume, Christian Lo Russo, Louis Schoorl, DNA Songs, Trinity and Hayley Warner.
response.summary_2: The artist who released I'm Still Here is Mindy McCready. The provided passages note McCready's 2010 album I'm Still Here includes the single "I'm Still Here" and re-recordings of her 1996 singles "Guys Do It All the Time" and "Ten Thousand Angels," but they do not state who wrote the debut song on her 1996 debut album.
